scenario: FEEL list items = ["a", "b", "c"]. Team wonders items[1] returns "a" or "b".
question: FEEL list indexing -- 0-based or 1-based?

options.text: FEEL list indexing is 1-based per the DMN specification, not 0-based as in JavaScript, Python, or Java arrays. For the list ["a", "b", "c"], items[1] returns "a" (the first element), items[2] returns "b", and items[3] returns "c". The index items[0] is invalid in FEEL and evaluates to null rather than the first element, which can silently hide off-by-one bugs for developers transitioning from 0-based languages. Some FEEL engines support negative indices (items[-1] for last element) but this should be verified per version. FEEL follows mathematical and spreadsheet convention rather than programming language convention for indexing.

FEEL list indexing is definitively 1-based per the DMN specification, not configurable or implementation-dependent. This is not a matter of two valid interpretations; it is a fixed part of the FEEL language specification. items[1] always returns the first element and items[0] returns null (invalid index) in any compliant FEEL engine including Camunda 8's. The 1-based convention follows spreadsheet and mathematical tradition, distinguishing FEEL from programming languages like JavaScript or Python that use 0-based indexing.

FEEL list indexing is 1-based per DMN spec: items[1] returns the first element "a", items[2] returns "b", items[3] returns "c". The index items[0] is invalid and returns null. This 1-based convention is inherited from mathematical and spreadsheet tradition and is standard across all compliant FEEL engines. Developers coming from 0-based languages (JavaScript, Python, Java arrays) must explicitly adjust their mental model when writing FEEL expressions, as items[0] will silently return null rather than throwing an error, potentially hiding bugs.

FEEL list indexing convention is not a runtime configuration or engine option; it is fixed by the DMN FEEL specification as 1-based. All compliant FEEL engines, including Camunda 8's built-in evaluator, implement 1-based indexing without any configuration option. Allowing configurable indexing base would make DMN expressions non-portable between engines and violate the specification. Organizations that need to interface FEEL with 0-based data structures must account for the offset in their expressions explicitly.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL list indexing: 1-based convention per DMN spec: `feel ["a", "b", "c"][1] → "a" (first) ["a", "b", "c"][2] → "b" (second) ["a", "b", "c"][3] → "c" (third) ["a", "b", "c"][4] → null (out of bounds; may error) ["a", "b", "c"][0] → null (invalid; may error) Why 1-based: DMN FEEL inherits from spreadsheet conventions: 1-based common in business. Distinguishes from programming: BPMN/DMN for business users. Common pitfalls: For developers from 0-based languages: Easy mistake: write items[0] expecting first. Result: null / error. Adjust mental model: +1 vs typical. Negative indexing: Some FEEL implementations: feel items[-1] → last element (per implementation) items[-2] → second-to-last Verify per version. Range indexing: feel items[1..2] → ["a", "b"] items[2..3] → ["b", "c"] Slice notation; same 1-based convention. For accessing last element: Pattern 1: count-based: feel items[count(items)] → last element Pattern 2: negative index (if supported): feel items[-1] For empty lists: feel [] → empty list [][1] → null count([]) → 0 For nested: feel matrix = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]] matrix[1] → [1, 2] matrix[1][2] → 2 Chained indexing. In Decision Tables: Input entries don't use indexing typically. Output expressions may use for accessing list items. For for-loops: feel for item in items return item Iterates all elements; no explicit indexing. For filter expressions: feel items[item = "a"] → filtered list with matching Predicate-based; not index-based. For specific position by predicate: feel items[item.priority = "high"][1] → first high-priority item Combined filter + index. In BPMN context: Same FEEL rules apply. Input Mapping / Output Mapping: 1-based indexing. Best practices: Remember 1-based: explicit comment if helpful. Defensive on bounds: check count before index. Avoid magic numbers: prefer named indices. Anti-patterns: Assuming 0-based: bugs. Index without bounds check: null surprises. Hardcoded indices: brittle. For
